issue: Transparency and accountability of nuclear arsenals and doctrines

entries.position_summary: The country calls for abolishing nuclear sharing and extended deterrence arrangements, withdrawing all nuclear weapons deployed overseas, and ending forward deployment of strategic forces. It further urges participants in such arrangements to clarify their practices, signaling a demand for greater transparency.
entries.quote: Fifth, nuclear-weapon States should abolish “nuclear sharing” and “extended deterrence” arrangements, withdraw all nuclear weapons deployed overseas back to their home countries, and cease the playing with fire behavior of forward deployment of strategic forces. Countries participating in “nuclear sharing” and “extended deterrence” arrangements should clarify whether

entries.position_summary: China supports discussing nuclear risk reduction and transparency as voluntary, security-sensitive intermediate measures, tailored to differing national circumstances. It opposes making these measures the core of the NPT review cycle or allowing them to substitute for the ultimate goal of nuclear disarmament under Article VI.
entries.quote: Fourth, it is necessary to take a right approach of intermediate measures for nuclear disarmament. Some countries have promoted nuclear risk reduction and nuclear transparency as intermediate measures for nuclear disarmament, which can be discussed, but they cannot become the core of the current review cycle, still less can they weaken or even substitute the goal of nuclear disarmament. China supports discussions on nuclear risk reduction and nuclear transparency on a voluntary basis without undermining national security, taking into account differences in the security environment, size of nuclear forces and nuclear strategies among countries.

entries.position_summary: The country urges protection of nuclear-weapon-free zones and calls on nuclear-weapon States to diminish the role of nuclear weapons, adopt no-first-use, avoid targeting specific countries, and lower alert levels. It demands ending nuclear sharing and extended deterrence and withdrawing nuclear weapons deployed abroad, with greater transparency about such arrangements and clarification of their compatibility with NPT Articles I and II. It also supports negotiating a legal instrument on security assurances and clarifying the rights and obligations of non-nuclear-weapon States involved in sharing arrangements.
entries.quote: Nuclear-weapon States and non-nuclear-weapon States should all refrain from taking actions that would undermine or damage the position of nuclear-weapon-free zones. (d) Nuclear-weapon States should diminish the role of nuclear weapons in their national security policies, abandon the nuclear deterrence policy based on the first use of nuclear weapons, refrain from making tailored nuclear deterrence policy against other countries, refrain from listing any country as the target of nuclear strike, reduce the alert status, and refrain from targeting nuclear weapons at any other country. (e)The relevant nuclear-weapon State should abandon the arrangement of nuclear sharing and extended deterrence, and withdraw all nuclear weapons deployed abroad back to its own territories. Before achieving this goal, the relevant nuclear- weapon States and non-nuclear-weapon States should be transparent about the arrangements above, and clarify whether they have breached Article 1 and 2 of the NPT. Meanwhile, in the context of concluding the international legal instrument on security assurances, the international community should have in-depth discussions on the rights and obligations of non- nuclear-weapon States participating in the arrangements of nuclear sharing and extended deterrence. 3/3

entries.position_summary: Supports voluntary transparency measures by nuclear-weapon States to build mutual trust, provided such steps do not compromise national security. Emphasizes a non-binding, security-conscious approach to transparency and confidence building.
entries.quote: Nuclear-weapon States should commit to adopting nuclear transparency measures and enhancing mutual trust on a voluntary basis without undermining national security.

entries.position_summary: The statement urges nuclear-weapon States to fully implement Article VI, adhere to disarmament commitments agreed at past NPT Review Conferences, and publicly declare they do not intend to retain nuclear weapons permanently.
entries.quote: 3. Nuclear-weapon States should commit themselves to the effective implementation of Article 6 of the NPT, abide by consensuses on nuclear disarmament reached in previous NPT Review Conferences, and openly declare that they have no intention to seek permanent possession of nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The statement asserts that transparency and confidence-building measures are not substitutes for genuine nuclear disarmament. It calls on states involved in nuclear sharing and extended deterrence to assume stronger nuclear transparency responsibilities.
entries.quote: Meanwhile, transparency and confidence-building measures cannot replace nuclear disarmament. Countries participating in the so-called nuclear sharing and extended deterrence arrangements should take corresponding nuclear transparency responsibilities.

entries.position_summary: China calls for a legally binding international instrument providing unconditional negative security assurances—no use or threat of use of nuclear weapons—toward non-nuclear-weapon States and Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zones. It urges the Conference on Disarmament to begin negotiations promptly and, pending such an instrument, calls on all nuclear-weapon States to publicly pledge these assurances.
entries.quote: 6. Given the above reasons, China strongly calls for conclusion of an international legal instrument as soon as possible on not using or threatening to use nuclear weapons against non-nuclear-weapon States or nuclear- weapon-free-zones. Therefore, China proposes an in-depth discussions on the following issues in the 11th Review Conference of NPT and its Preparatory Committee Meetings, and seek consensus on the above issues to be included in the outcome documents. (a) The Conference on Disarmament (CD) should start substantive work as soon as possible on concluding an international legal instrument on not using or threatening to use nuclear weapons against non-nuclear-weapon States or nuclear-weapon-free- zones. (b) Before concluding the international legal instrument, all of the nuclear-weapon States should make public statements to unconditionally undertake not to use or threaten to use nuclear 2/3

entries.position_summary: The country supports preserving and expanding attention to cross-cutting issues—transparency and reporting, education and capacity-building, and gender and diversity—within the NPT context. It proposes dedicating plenary sessions at Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committee to address these topics.
entries.quote: • Preserving and potentially expanding opportunities to address cross-cutting issues, such as transparency, reporting, education and capacity-building, gender and diversity. These could be addressed in dedicated plenary sessions of review conferences and the Preparatory Committee.

entries.position_summary: China urges the P5 to further reduce strategic risks, asserting that nuclear weapons should never be used and that the use or threat of use should be opposed. It supports building on the P5 joint statement to prevent nuclear war, while arguing that differing nuclear forces and policies mean there are no one-size-fits-all transparency or risk-reduction measures.
entries.quote: Secondly, strive to reduce strategic risks. Against the backdrop of severe and complex challenges facing the current international security situation, China unequivocally holds that nuclear weapons should never be used and nuclear war must never be fought, and that the international community should jointly oppose the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons. China calls on the five nuclear-weapon States (P5) to make further efforts to reduce strategic risks on the basis of the Joint Statement of the Leaders of the Five nuclear-weapon States on Preventing Nuclear War and Avoiding Nuclear Arms Races issued in January last year. At the same time, it should be pointed out that the nuclear forces, nuclear policies, and security environment of nuclear-weapon States are very different, and there are no universally applicable nuclear transparency and nuclear risk reduction measures.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights its consistent national reporting on NPT implementation using the NWS reporting format and notes the growing practice among States parties. It supports dedicating time at formal PrepCom and Review Conference meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports to enhance transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: 8. The United States has reported consistently on its own implementation of the Non-Proliferation Treaty and actions to strengthen the Treaty regime across all three pillars, including to the 2014 Preparatory Conference, the 2015 Review Conference and, most recently, the 2022 Review Conference (NPT/CONF.2020/57), using a reporting format adopted by the nuclear-weapon States. The practice of submitting national reports has grown steadily and is now well established. In 2015, 22 States parties submitted national reports, and in 2022, 40 States parties did so. These reports have usually been presented in side events, as there has not been place for such presentations at formal meetings of the Preparatory Committee or the Review Conference. The United States would support dedicating time at those formal meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports. Such discussions would provide an enhanced mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges the Preparatory Committee to adopt a package of measures to improve the NPT review process, building on the Chair’s working paper. It also supports advancing the near-adopted proposal on transparency and accountability to consolidate emerging consensus from the working group.
entries.quote: 16. The United States looks forward to discussion of each of these issues, as well as proposals put forward by other States parties, at the upcoming Preparatory Committee meeting. This meeting offers an opportunity for States parties to consolidate the consensus that was nearly achieved in the working group by adopting a package of measures to improve the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process based on the Chair’s working paper and the alternative proposal on transparency and accountability that was nearly adopted at the end of the working group meeting. 24-09063 5/5

entries.position_summary: China emphasizes its coordination role in the P5 process, promoting dialogue and expert meetings among the five nuclear-weapon states to increase transparency of nuclear policies, reduce misunderstandings, and enhance strategic stability. It pledges to continue pragmatic dialogue and cooperation among the P5.
entries.quote: In the face of the geopolitical crisis and the lack of mutual trust among major countries, China has actively fulfilled its responsibilities as the coordinator of P5 mechanism, promoted dialogue and exchanges among the five nuclear-weapon States, and held nuclear policy expert meetings to enhance understanding of each other’s nuclear policies, which will help avoid misunderstanding and miscalculation and promote strategic stability among major countries. China will continue to actively promote dialogue and cooperation among the five countries in a pragmatic manner.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates regular reporting on implementation of the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI and urges nuclear-weapon States to use standardized reporting formats and intervals, reinforcing the strengthened NPT review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: The country supports a step-by-step approach to disarmament based on strategic stability and undiminished security, assigning primary responsibility to states with the largest arsenals to reduce first. It urges continued implementation and deepening of New START reductions. It opposes imposing identical disarmament and transparency obligations on all nuclear-armed states given differing doctrines and stockpiles.
entries.quote: Nuclear weapon is a product of history, and therefore nuclear disarmament is naturally a historical process, which should follow the principles of “maintaining global strategic stability” and “undiminished security for all”, and be advanced in a step-by-step manner. To require countries with vastly different nuclear doctrines and nuclear stockpiles to fulfill the same nuclear disarmament and transparency obligations is not in line with the logic of history and reality, and will surely lead the international nuclear disarmament process to a dead end. The priority now is that the countries with the largest nuclear arsenals should fulfil their special and primary responsibilities for nuclear disarmament, continue to effectively implement the New START Treaty and further reduce their nuclear arsenals in a significant and substantive manner, so as to create the conditions for other nuclear-weapon States to join the nuclear disarmament ─ 8 ─

entries.position_summary: The country urges participants in nuclear sharing and extended deterrence to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in their security doctrines and to clarify the arrangements’ compliance with NPT Articles I and II. It calls on relevant nuclear-weapon States to withdraw nuclear weapons deployed abroad, end and not replicate nuclear-sharing arrangements, and cease nuclear exercises and nuclear information exchanges with non-nuclear-weapon States.
entries.quote: 7. Nuclear-weapon States and non-nuclear-weapon States participating in the so-called nuclear sharing and extended deterrence arrangements should take concrete measures to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in their national and collective security doctrines, clarify that whether these arrangements are complying with the Article I and II of NPT. Relevant nuclear-weapon States should withdrawing their nuclear weapons deployed overseas, ceasing and not replicating nuclear-sharing arrangements, not conducting nuclear exercises or exchanging nuclear information with non-nuclear-weapon States.

entries.position_summary: The United States backs a simplified, near-consensus proposal to enhance transparency by dedicating plenary time in PrepComs and the Review Conference to consider States parties’ national reports, especially those of nuclear-weapon States. It sees this procedural change as the best chance to secure consensus on transparency and accountability in the review cycle.
entries.quote: 10. The working group held extensive and constructive deliberations on proposals for transparency and accountability, focused on interactive discussion of national reports by States parties. Paragraphs 16 to 19 of the Chair’s working paper address this issue in detail, but fall short of consensus. A simplified alternative proposal submitted by four States parties came very close to achieving consensus, with only one State party objecting. The United States believes that this alternative, reproduced below, represents the best chance of gaining consensus in this area: The working group recommends that the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the President of the Review Conference dedicate time in plenary sessions of the review cycle to consider the national reports of States parties, in particular the reports of the nuclear-weapon States, with regard to the 24-09063 3/5

entries.position_summary: China urges the P5 to fully implement NPT obligations and strengthen coordination to support and improve the NPT review process. It backs enhanced P5 nuclear policy exchanges and strategic risk-reduction dialogues, including on missile defense, outer space, cyberspace, and AI, to bolster transparency and strategic stability.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, China believes that the five nuclear-weapon States should work together to maintain the integrity and effectiveness of the NPT mechanism, and work together to fully implement all NPT obligations. For a long time, the P5 mechanism has played a unique and positive role in the NPT review process, and should continue to make its special contributions to such process. China has been actively promoting dialogue and cooperation among the P5 with practical actions, and advocates that the P5 further strengthen coordination and cooperation in promoting the NPT review process. China actively participates in nuclear policy exchanges among the P5 and dialogues on reducing strategic risks. On the basis of mutual respect and equality, China is willing to further the in-depth dialogue within the P5 mechanism, focusing on broad issues affecting strategic stability, such as missile defense systems, outer space, cyberspace, and artificial intelligence. 4

entries.position_summary: China affirms a defensive, minimum-deterrence posture, pledges unconditional no-first-use, and offers negative security assurances to non-nuclear-weapon states and nuclear-weapon-free zones. It opposes arms racing, claims policy stability as meaningful transparency, and advocates the complete prohibition and elimination of nuclear weapons in support of disarmament objectives.
entries.quote: China was compelled to develop nuclear weapons during a particular time in history, in order to deal with nuclear threat, break the nuclear monopoly and prevent nuclear war. From the day it came into possession of such weapons, China has advocated their complete prohibition and thorough destruction, and has stayed committed to the no-first-use of nuclear weapons at anytime and under any circumstance and unconditionally undertakes not to use or threaten to use nuclear weapons against non-nuclear-weapon States or nuclear-weapon-free zones. China has always pursued a defensive nuclear strategy and kept its military development at the minimum level required for national security. China has no intention of pursuing arms race with any country. For the past six decades, China's understanding of the nature of nuclear weapons has remained unchanged. China's nuclear policy is highly stable, consistent and predictable, and is the most meaningful transparency. China's nuclear policy in itself is an important contribution to the cause of international nuclear disarmament and non-proliferation. Some countries deliberately misinterpret or even smear China's nuclear policy.

entries.position_summary: The country supports improving the NPT review process by carefully organizing sessions, curating the mix of national reports, structuring and moderating discussions, and facilitating Q&A. It favors submitting written questions in advance to enhance efficiency and accountability.
entries.quote: • The organization of each session, including the number and mix of national reports, the structure and moderation of the discussion and answering questions from other States parties, should be considered carefully. Given the time constraints, providing questions in advance in writing could help to structure the discussion.

entries.position_summary: The country urges nuclear-weapon States to implement the P5 Joint Statement to prevent nuclear war, avoid arms races, and reaffirm that their nuclear weapons are not targeted at any state. It calls for sustained dialogue on strategic risk reduction and on nuclear policies and doctrines, and for avoiding military confrontation.
entries.quote: 5. Nuclear-weapon States should make their utmost endeavors to prevent nuclear wars. Discussions should focus on implementing the Joint Statement of the Leaders of the Five Nuclear-Weapon States on Preventing Nuclear War and Avoiding Arms Races, reaffirming that none of their nuclear weapons are targeted at each other or at any other State, maintaining dialogue and communication on strategic risks reduction and nuclear policies and doctrines, and avoiding military confrontation and arms races.
